examples.title: Binary Search in Single-Level Index
examples.description: Algorithm to search for a key value using a dense index with binary search
examples.code:
ALGORITHM IndexSearch(indexArray, targetKey)
BEGIN
  left = 0
  right = length(indexArray) - 1
  
  WHILE left <= right DO
    mid = (left + right) / 2
    
    IF indexArray[mid].key == targetKey THEN
      RETURN indexArray[mid].recordPointer
    ELSE IF indexArray[mid].key < targetKey THEN
      left = mid + 1
    ELSE
      right = mid - 1
  END WHILE
  
  RETURN NULL  // Key not found
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm performs binary search on a sorted dense index array. The index entries contain key values and pointers to actual data records. Time complexity is O(log n) where n is the number of index entries.

examples.title: B+ Tree Node Structure Implementation
examples.description: Python implementation showing B+ tree leaf node structure with keys and data pointers
examples.code:
class BPlusTreeLeafNode:
    def __init__(self, order):
        self.order = order  # Maximum keys per node
        self.keys = []      # Sorted keys
        self.data = []      # Data pointers
        self.next = None    # Pointer to next leaf node
        self.is_leaf = True
    
    def insert(self, key, value):
        # Insert key-value pair maintaining sorted order
        i = 0
        while i < len(self.keys) and self.keys[i] < key:
            i += 1
        self.keys.insert(i, key)
        self.data.insert(i, value)
        
        # Check for overflow
        if len(self.keys) > self.order:
            return self.split()
        return None
    
    def split(self):
        # Create new leaf and distribute keys
        new_node = BPlusTreeLeafNode(self.order)
        mid = len(self.keys) // 2
        
        new_node.keys = self.keys[mid:]
        new_node.data = self.data[mid:]
        self.keys = self.keys[:mid]
        self.data = self.data[:mid]
        
        # Link the leaf nodes
        new_node.next = self.next
        self.next = new_node
        
        return (self.keys[-1], new_node)
examples.explanation: This code implements a B+ tree leaf node with key-data pairs and a linked list pointer to the next leaf. The split method handles node overflow during insertion, creating a new leaf and distributing entries evenly.

examples.title: Multi-Level Index Search Algorithm
examples.description: C implementation demonstrating multi-level index search traversal through hierarchy
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates multi-level index traversal. Starting from the top-level index block, it uses binary search at each level to narrow down to the next index block until reaching the leaf level that points to actual data records.
examples.timeComplexity: O(logₖ(n)) where k is block size

examples.title: Index Selection Algorithm for Query Optimization
examples.description: Algorithm showing how DBMS optimizer decides whether to use an index based on selectivity
examples.code:
ALGORITHM DetermineIndexUsage(query, availableIndexes)
BEGIN
  selectedPlan = NULL
  minCost = INFINITY
  
  FOR EACH index IN availableIndexes DO
    // Check if index can be used for WHERE clause
    IF index.column IN query.whereColumns THEN
      // Calculate selectivity
      selectivity = index.distinctValues / table.rowCount
      
      // Estimate number of index entries to retrieve
      indexEntries = table.rowCount * selectivity
      
      // Calculate I/O cost
      indexIO = index.treeHeight
      dataIO = indexEntries * index.clusteringFactor
      
      totalCost = indexIO + dataIO
      
      // Compare with full table scan cost
      fullScanCost = table.rowCount * table.pageCount
      
      IF totalCost < fullScanCost AND totalCost < minCost THEN
        minCost = totalCost
        selectedPlan = CREATE_INDEX_SCAN(index, selectivity)
      END IF
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  // If no index beneficial, use full table scan
  IF selectedPlan == NULL THEN
    RETURN CREATE_TABLE_SCAN()
  END IF
  
  RETURN selectedPlan
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm simulates how a database query optimizer evaluates different access methods. It calculates the cost of using each available index by considering selectivity, tree height, and clustering factor, then compares with full table scan to choose the optimal execution plan.
examples.timeComplexity: O(m * log n) where m is number of indexes
